summary: The article addresses the common problem of organizations being trapped in cycles of incomplete legacy system replacements. The authors identify that technology is only 50% of the legacy problem—ways of working, organization structure, and leadership are equally important. They propose breaking the cycle through four key activities: understanding desired outcomes, breaking problems into smaller parts, successfully delivering those parts, and changing the organization to allow ongoing improvement. The piece emphasizes that without broader organizational change, companies should expect the same failed outcomes they've seen previously. A detailed case study demonstrates how these patterns were successfully applied to replace critical integration middleware.
key_points: Technology replacement alone fails because organizations leave leadership, structure, and ways of working unchanged while expecting different outcomes

Legacy modernization must balance five potential outcomes: reducing cost of change, improving business processes, retiring old systems, responding to imminent disruption, and adopting newer technology
Finding seams in both business and technical architecture enables breaking monolithic replacement into smaller, independently deliverable parts
Transitional architecture patterns like Event Interception, Legacy Mimic, and Branch by Abstraction allow incremental migration while maintaining business operations
Corporate antibodies—existing processes and middle management—actively work to reject new ways of working even when leadership supports change
money_quotes: Technology is at most only 50% of the legacy problem, ways of working, organization structure and leadership are just as important to success.
If you just change the legacy and do nothing else it is fair to expect you'll be replacing legacy again within a few years.
At some point during each programme they hit a crisis point where changing business needs would overtake their current tech strategy and hence trigger the need to start over.

A common objection is that finding these seams is too difficult. While we agree it is challenging at first, we have found it to be a better approach than the alternatives which all too often result in Feature Parity and Big Bang releases.
Legacy is also the product of an organization's culture and leadership, without broader change you should expect the same outcomes as seen previously.
Selecting a huge platform with a 5-10 years pay back time is hard to justify when we acknowledge this accelerating pace of change.
We've been shocked over the years at how many large organizations are running their businesses on unsupported hardware and software, buying spare parts on eBay is surprisingly common story to hear.
key_insight: Legacy system replacement fails not primarily due to technology choices but because organizations attempt to change technology while leaving their structure, culture, and ways of working intact—creating a perpetual treadmill of incomplete modernization efforts.
